bibliographic_data.title: Demonstration Notebook: Finding the Most Suited In-Context Learning Example from Interactions
bibliographic_data.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) benefit greatly from prompt engineering, with in-context learning standing as a pivotal technique. While former approaches have provided various ways to construct the demonstrations used for in-context learning, they often ignore the inherent heterogeneity within datasets, applying the same demonstrations to all reasoning questions. This paper proposes a novel prompt engineering workflow built around a new object called the 'demonstration notebook.' The notebook helps identify the most suitable in-context learning example for a question by gathering and reusing information from the LLM’s past interactions. Our experiments show that this approach outperforms all existing methods for automatic demonstration construction and selection, achieving state-of-the-art results on several reasoning benchmarks.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper introduces a novel prompt engineering method called the 'demonstration notebook' that automatically constructs and selects demonstrations tailored to each question, outperforming existing methods.
core_content.key_methodology: A demonstration notebook is proposed which includes three key components: a demonstration set, an interaction record set, and a noted question set. The collection phase consists of several epochs with procedures for demonstration expansion, on-policy and off-policy collection, and pruning. A prompter is trained to select the most suitable demonstration from the set.
outcomes: The approach outperforms all existing methods for automatic demonstration construction and selection.
State-of-the-art results are achieved on several reasoning benchmarks.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This paper addresses the challenge of automatically creating and choosing demonstrations tailored to each question in large language models (LLMs). It proposes a novel prompt engineering workflow called the 'demonstration notebook.' The notebook helps identify the most suitable in-context learning example for a question by gathering and reusing information from the LLM’s past interactions. Experiments show that this approach outperforms existing methods, achieving state-of-the-art results on several reasoning benchmarks. Additionally, the paper contributes a rigorous analysis method to reveal the 'demonstrative regime' of demonstrations, providing insights into how they relate to different question types.
